skill.name: voice-ai-development
skill.description: Build low-latency voice AI applications with OpenAI Realtime API, Vapi, Deepgram, ElevenLabs, LiveKit, and WebRTC patterns.
skill.summary: Guides real-time voice agent design, streaming audio pipelines, and provider selection for production voice AI apps.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static findings were reviewed in context. The external command and weak cryptography alerts are false positives caused by Markdown code fences, inline code, and ordinary prose. The API key findings are placeholder credentials in documentation examples, not embedded secrets, but users should avoid copying hardcoded key patterns into production.

low_findings.title: Provider API Key Placeholders in Examples
low_findings.description: The snippets show placeholder API keys and authorization headers for voice AI providers. No real secret is present, but production users should load keys from secret storage.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged lines clearly contain credential placeholders and bearer-token construction. Confidence is below very high because the values are examples, not live secrets.

low_findings.title: Markdown Backticks Misidentified as Command Execution
low_findings.description: The flagged backticks are Markdown code fence delimiters or inline code references. No shell command execution or Ruby backtick execution is present.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Each flagged location is a Markdown delimiter or inline reference in SKILL.md. No executable script context or command invocation exists.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Pattern False Positive
low_findings.description: The flagged lines contain description text, a capability bullet, a weather tool description, and a Vapi comment. No hash, cipher, or cryptographic API is present.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The surrounding context shows ordinary voice AI documentation, not cryptographic operations. No evidence found for weak algorithm usage.

content.value_statement: Voice applications fail when latency, audio quality, and provider choices are handled late. This skill guides real-time voice agents using OpenAI, Vapi, Deepgram, ElevenLabs, LiveKit, and WebRTC.
actual_capabilities: Designs low-latency voice agent architectures with streaming audio paths.
Explains when to use OpenAI Realtime API, Vapi, Deepgram, ElevenLabs, LiveKit, or WebRTC.
Provides implementation patterns for speech recognition, synthesis, webhooks, and call flows.
Recommends latency controls such as interim transcription, streaming TTS, and barge-in handling.
Identifies common voice AI anti-patterns that harm conversation quality.
limitations: Does not include a complete runnable application template.
Uses placeholder credentials that must be replaced with secure configuration.
Provider APIs, model names, and SDK behavior may change over time.
Does not provide compliance guidance for call recording or transcript retention.

use_cases.title: Prototype a Web Voice Assistant
use_cases.description: Plan a browser-based assistant that uses real-time audio, turn detection, and streaming responses.

use_cases.title: Build a Phone Support Agent
use_cases.description: Create a Vapi-based call agent with assistant setup, webhook handling, and call event processing.

use_cases.title: Optimize a Custom Voice Pipeline
use_cases.description: Choose separate STT, LLM, and TTS providers and reduce latency across the full conversation loop.

prompt_templates.title: Choose a Voice Stack
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me choose a voice AI stack for my use case. I need real-time conversation, expected call volume, budget limits, and deployment target.

prompt_templates.title: Design a Realtime Session
prompt_templates.prompt: Design an OpenAI Realtime API session for my voice app. Include audio formats, turn detection settings, tool calls, and latency tradeoffs.

prompt_templates.title: Plan a Vapi Agent
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a Vapi voice agent for customer support. Include assistant behavior, voice provider, transcriber settings, webhook events, and failure handling.

prompt_templates.title: Audit Latency and Interruptions
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my voice AI architecture for latency, barge-in support, provider lock-in, transcript handling, and audio quality risks.

output_examples.input: I need a fast voice assistant for a web app.
output: Recommended architecture: browser audio capture, WebRTC transport, real-time model session, and streaming playback.
Key settings: server voice activity detection, short silence windows, and immediate playback of generated audio.
Main risk: latency spikes if audio is buffered before model processing.

output_examples.input: I want a phone support agent that can check order status.
output: Recommended approach: use Vapi for telephony, a support-oriented assistant prompt, and a webhook for order lookup.
Webhook events should validate tool arguments, return concise results, and log call outcomes.
Protect customer data with authentication, retention limits, and transcript access controls.

output_examples.input: My voice agent feels slow and users interrupt it.
output: Likely causes: non-streaming transcription, delayed synthesis, long silence detection, or queued audio playback.
Fixes: enable interim results, stream LLM output into TTS, detect barge-in, and clear the audio queue.
Measure end-to-end latency from speech start to audible response.

best_practices: Stream audio, model output, and speech synthesis wherever provider support allows it.
Store provider credentials outside source code and rotate them when access changes.
Design barge-in, silence detection, and transcript handling before production launch.
anti_patterns: Building a non-streaming pipeline that waits for every stage to finish.
Ignoring interruptions while the assistant continues speaking.
Choosing one provider for every layer without testing latency, quality, and failure modes.

faq.question: What type of voice AI work does this skill support?
faq.answer: It supports architecture and implementation planning for real-time agents, phone agents, transcription, synthesis, and WebRTC audio flows.

faq.question: Does it create a complete application?
faq.answer: No. It provides patterns and guidance that developers can adapt to their own application code.

faq.question: Which providers are covered?
faq.answer: It covers OpenAI Realtime API, Vapi, Deepgram, ElevenLabs, LiveKit, and general WebRTC concepts.

faq.question: Can it help reduce voice latency?
faq.answer: Yes. It focuses on streaming, interim results, turn detection, barge-in handling, and provider selection.

faq.question: Are the API keys in the examples real?
faq.answer: No. They are placeholders. Use environment variables or a secrets manager for real provider credentials.

faq.question: Does it cover privacy or legal compliance?
faq.answer: Only at a high level. Teams should review call recording, consent, retention, and transcript access requirements separately.
